confirmed.title: score_work_product can be re-run on any work product, creating duplicate transfer evidence and orphaning the prior score
confirmed.description: score_work_product() validates rubric presence, case existence, node resolution and score ranges, but never checks work_product.status. Calling it again on a product already in status 'scored', 'revision-requested', 'accepted' or 'withdrawn' unconditionally (a) creates a NEW Attempt + a NEW transfer EvidenceRecord via create_evidence_record (lines 379-404), (b) overwrites work_product.rubric_score_id with the new score, orphaning the previous RubricScore, and (c) forces work_product.status back to 'scored' (line 421), silently clobbering a 'revision-requested' or 'accepted' state. I confirmed this dynamically: scoring the same work product twice produced 2 EvidenceRecords and 2 RubricScores for the learner, with rubric_score_id pointing at the second and the first orphaned. Because every score emits a transfer EvidenceRecord into the same ledger that mastery/capability estimators read (mastery/service.py, capability/repository.py), repeated or accidental re-scoring inflates a learner's observed-evidence history and corrupts downstream mastery and capability scores. Tests only cover a single scoring (tests/cases/test_work_products.py:88 asserts exactly 1 evidence record), so the re-score path is unguarded and untested.
confirmed.suggested_fix: At the top of score_work_product, guard the state transition: only allow scoring when work_product.status in ('submitted','draft') (or an explicitly allowed 'revision-requested' re-score path), else raise ValueError('work product is not in a scoreable state'). If re-scoring must be supported, first delete/supersede the existing EvidenceRecord and RubricScore referenced by work_product.rubric_score_id instead of leaving them orphaned, so the evidence ledger stays consistent.
confirmed.cluster: Core learning-object model (cases, competencies, mastery, capability)

verdict.reasoning:
The core factual claims are all verified. score_work_product (src/lms/cases/repository.py:326-422) has no work_product.status guard — it only validates rubric presence, case existence, node resolution, and score ranges. WORK_PRODUCT_STATUSES (src/lms/cases/models.py:35-42) includes scored/revision-requested/accepted/withdrawn, so re-scoring a terminal product is structurally possible. I confirmed the behavior dynamically with a throwaway probe test (which passed): scoring the same work product twice yields 2 distinct EvidenceRecords and 2 distinct RubricScores; work_product.rubric_score_id repoints to the second score, leaving the first RubricScore and its EvidenceRecord orphaned (no FK references the first score); and status is forced back to 'scored' (line 421), which would clobber a 'revision-requested' or 'accepted' state.

The downstream-corruption claim is also substantiated: src/lms/mastery/service.py:41-43 selects ALL EvidenceRecords for a learner grouped by node with no deduplication, and src/lms/mastery/policy.py:29 computes confidence = min(1.0, 0.35 + len(records)*0.12), so duplicate observed-evidence records directly inflate confidence and the reported evidence_count. The capability layer likewise consumes observed evidence. The codebase even uses supersession semantics elsewhere (src/lms/feedback/repository.py:328 sets prior.status='superseded'), so leaving prior scores orphaned is inconsistent with the established pattern. The revision loop (src/lms/feedback/repository.py:346 sets status='revision-requested') creates a legitimate re-score path that would hit this unguarded behavior.

I could not refute it: there is no validation elsewhere, no guard, no test covering the re-score path (tests/cases/test_work_products.py:88 asserts exactly 1 evidence record for the single-score case only), and it is not intended behavior.
verdict.notes: Downgraded from high to medium on reachability. score_work_product is NOT exposed via any HTTP endpoint or UI route — src/lms/cases/api.py only exposes submit/list/get work-product routes, and the only non-test callers are the thin service re-export (src/lms/cases/service.py) and the repository itself. So the "any work product can be re-run" framing overstates real-world exposure: today it is an internal library function with no user-triggerable path, so there is no accidental or unauthenticated trigger surface in production. The defect is genuine (orphaned RubricScore + duplicate observed-evidence inflating mastery confidence/capability + clobbered terminal status, all untested), but current blast radius is limited by the lack of any caller wiring it to an endpoint. The suggested fix is sound: add a status guard allowing scoring only from submitted/draft (plus an explicit revision-requested re-score path that supersedes the prior RubricScore + EvidenceRecord rather than orphaning them), matching the supersession pattern already used in feedback/repository.py.

confirmed.title: Spaced-repetition success ramp is frozen at step 0 (always 1 day) because no code ever marks queue items 'completed'
confirmed.description: The core spacing interval is chosen by `_success_interval_days(prior_successes)` over the ramp SUCCESS_INTERVALS_DAYS = (1, 3, 7, 14, 28). `prior_successes` comes from `_count_prior_successful_reviews`, which counts `ReviewQueueItem.status == "completed"` for due-review/new-learning items. However, NO production code path ever sets a ReviewQueueItem status to 'completed'. A repo-wide search shows the only 'completed' assignment is for a FeedbackAction (feedback/repository.py:404); there is no review-queue dispatch/complete endpoint (scheduling/api.py only exposes GET for the queue), and neither the attempt UI (ui/attempts.py) nor the demo touches queue status. I reproduced this with a real DB session: four consecutive successful reviews of the same node all emit rule='success-ramp-step-0' with prior_successes=0, so every successful review is rescheduled at 1 day forever. The 3/7/14/28-day expansion that defines spaced retrieval never happens for any real learner. The only test that exercises advancement (tests/scheduling/test_review_queue.py:186 `test_success_ramp_extends_after_prior_completions`) manually sets `first.status = 'completed'` at line 195, which masks the gap because production has no equivalent step.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Decide what 'a prior successful review' means and count it from a source production actually writes. Two viable fixes: (a) Add a queue-item completion transition (e.g., a POST /review-queue/{id}/complete route + repository helper that sets status='completed' and schedule_state='completed') and call it when a review attempt is recorded, so `_count_prior_successful_reviews` has real data; or (b) Re-base `_count_prior_successful_reviews` on a source that already exists for each successful retrieval — e.g., count prior EvidenceRecords for the (learner, knowledge_node) whose classified signal is 'success' (excluding the current attempt), or count prior completed/scheduled ReviewSchedule rows. Add an end-to-end test that runs 2-3 real schedule_from_attempt cycles (no manual status edit) and asserts due_at advances 1 -> 3 -> 7 days.
confirmed.evidence: service.py:257 `ReviewQueueItem.status == "completed"`; repro output: cycle 0..3 all `rule=success-ramp-step-0 prior_successes_seen=0`. Grep: only FeedbackAction gets status='completed'.

verdict.reasoning: Verified the issue in full. (1) `_count_prior_successful_reviews` (src/lms/scheduling/service.py:247-261) counts `ReviewQueueItem.status == "completed"` scoped to reason_codes due-review/new-learning. (2) Repo-wide grep confirms NO production code ever writes a ReviewQueueItem status of "completed" (or "dispatched"): the repository's `create_review_queue_item` only sets the default "pending" and there is no update/dispatch/complete helper; the only `status = "completed"` assignment in src/ is for FeedbackAction (feedback/repository.py:404). (3) scheduling/api.py exposes only GET for the review queue (the sole POST is for remediation-triggers). (4) The attempt UI (ui/attempts.py) and ui/api.py consume the queue read-only via get_review_queue_overview and only render item.status; neither transitions it. (5) The one advancement test, tests/scheduling/test_review_queue.py:186, manually sets `first.status = "completed"` at line 195 — there is no production equivalent, so it masks the gap. I reproduced with a real DB session via the actual create_attempt + schedule_from_attempt flow: four consecutive successful reviews of the same (learner, node) all emit rule=success-ramp-step-0 with prior_successes_seen=0 and interval_days=1, and 0 items ever reach completed status. The 3/7/14/28-day ramp never fires for any real learner. Every element of the report is accurate and well-evidenced.
verdict.notes: Confirmed real, but downgraded from critical to high. It is a feature-correctness defect (spaced ramp permanently frozen at 1 day), not data loss/security/crash — items still schedule, just never expand. The QUEUE_STATUSES tuple defines "dispatched" and "completed" yet neither is ever written, indicating the review-completion lifecycle is unbuilt milestone scaffolding rather than a regression (the reporter's own fix (a) is "add a completion transition," i.e. new work). It remains high, not medium: this is the headline spaced-retrieval algorithm, the gap is invisible because the only advancement test fabricates the missing 'completed' state, and it directly undermines the Milestone-4 pre-registered day-30 retention gate (README:235). Single-user research project at Milestone 4-6 with no live learners caps real-world blast radius. Suggested fixes are sound; fix (b) (re-base on existing EvidenceRecords/ReviewSchedule rows) avoids inventing a new endpoint, and an end-to-end test asserting due_at advances 1->3->7 without manual status edits is the right regression guard.

confirmed.title: FSRS adapter rates hint/reference-supported high-score answers as 'good' instead of 'hard' (rule ordering bug)
confirmed.description: FSRS_RULES is evaluated in order and the first match wins (evidence_to_fsrs_rating, line 188). The `partial-at-mastery` rule (returns 'good', value 3) is placed at index 3, BEFORE `supported-or-low-confidence-correct` (returns 'hard', value 2) at index 5. `_is_score_at_mastery` only checks `correctness is not False and score >= 0.85` and does NOT inspect hint_used / reference_accessed / support_level. So an answer that was correct with a revealed hint and normalized_score=0.95 is rated 'good'/value 3, even though the explicit intent (rule_id `supported-or-low-confidence-correct`, reason text 'Correct evidence with support ... maps to FSRS Hard') is to treat any supported correct answer as 'hard'. I confirmed this empirically: support_level='hint', hint_used=True, normalized_score=0.95 -> label='good', value=3, rule_id='partial-at-mastery'. In schedule_from_attempt this is partially masked because `_conservative_signal` blends FSRS with `_classify_signal` (which catches support_level) and downgrades to low-confidence-success, but the FSRS rating label/value/rule_id are still recorded in decision_log and the SchedulerDecision audit row (service.py:704-709), so the Inspect/explainability surface reports a wrong, over-confident rating.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the partial-credit rules support-aware or reorder so support is checked first for correct answers. Simplest: in `_is_score_at_mastery` (and ideally `_is_score_under_mastery`) add `and not _has_support(record)` so supported answers fall through to the `supported-or-low-confidence-correct` rule; or move the `supported-or-low-confidence-correct` and low-confidence checks ahead of the partial-credit-by-score rules in FSRS_RULES. Add a test: correctness=True, support_level='hint', normalized_score=0.95 must yield label='hard', rule_id='supported-or-low-confidence-correct'.
confirmed.evidence: Repro: `supported+mastery FSRS -> good 3 partial-at-mastery`. fsrs_adapter.py:113 `record.correctness is not False and score is not None and score >= 0.85` (no support check).

verdict.reasoning:
Verified every claim against the code and reproduced it empirically.

RULE ORDERING (fsrs_adapter.py:116-183): Confirmed. In FSRS_RULES, `partial-at-mastery` (rating "good", value 3, index 3, lines 143-150) is evaluated BEFORE `supported-or-low-confidence-correct` (rating "hard", value 2, index 5, lines 159-166). evidence_to_fsrs_rating (line 188) returns the first match.

GUARD MISSING (line 111-113): Confirmed. `_is_score_at_mastery` checks only `record.correctness is not False and score is not None and score >= 0.85` with no support check — unlike `_is_fast_first_attempt` (line 84) and `_is_good_unsupported_correct` (line 95), which both call `not _has_support(record)`. So a supported correct answer with a high score never reaches the intended "hard" rule.

EMPIRICAL REPRO: Confirmed exactly. correctness=True, support_level='hint', hint_used=True, normalized_score=0.95 -> label='good', value=3, rule_id='partial-at-mastery'. The same record WITHOUT a score correctly yields label='hard', rule_id='supported-or-low-confidence-correct'. The mere presence of a score flips the rating in a way that contradicts the stated rule reason ("Correct evidence with support ... maps to FSRS Hard").

PERSISTENCE/SURFACE (service.py:701-756): Confirmed. The wrong fsrs_rating (label/value/rule_id) is written into decision_log and stored on both ReviewQueueItem and the SchedulerDecision audit row (models.py:98, 356), exposed via schemas.py:45 and 138 to the Inspect/explainability surface. The module docstring states the whole point is to record the FSRS label and rule_id "for the Inspect surface."

MASKING (service.py:677-691): Confirmed and correctly characterized. _classify_signal returns "low-confidence-success" for supported answers (line 499), and _conservative_signal takes the lower of that and the FSRS-derived "success", so the actual scheduling outcome (interval/priority) is conservative and NOT corrupted. The defect is confined to the explainability/audit field.

TEST GAP: Confirmed. No existing test combines a high score (>=0.85) with support. test_supported_correct_maps_to_hard uses support but no score; test_partial_credit_boundaries' "good" case has support_level default 'none'. All 14 existing adapter tests pass against the buggy code. The reporter's suggested test (correctness=True, support_level='hint', normalized_score=0.95 must yield 'hard'/'supported-or-low-confidence-correct') would correctly fail today.

I could not refute this. The suggested fix (add `and not _has_support(record)` to _is_score_at_mastery, and ideally _is_score_under_mastery) is correct and minimal.
verdict.notes: Real domain-fidelity / audit-correctness bug, severity confirmed medium (low end). The actual learner-facing scheduling behavior is NOT broken — _conservative_signal (service.py:687-691) reliably downgrades supported correct answers to "low-confidence-success", and no other consumer reads the FSRS rating value (the mastery policy in src/lms/mastery/policy.py is an independent placeholder). Impact is limited to the persisted explainability/audit trail (decision_log.fsrs_rating on ReviewQueueItem and SchedulerDecision), which records a self-contradictory over-confident rating: label="good"/rule_id="partial-at-mastery" while the scheduler simultaneously treats the same answer as low-confidence. Not upgraded to high because there is no scheduling or mastery miscalculation; not downgraded to low because it is a genuine correctness defect in an audit surface whose stated purpose is deterministic explainability, and it is entirely untested. Fix location: src/lms/scheduling/fsrs_adapter.py:111-113 (add `and not _has_support(record)`), with a regression test in tests/scheduling/test_fsrs_adapter.py.

confirmed.title: Markdown importer links H2 sections to the wrong file's node (cross-file prerequisite edges)
confirmed.description: In `_parse_heading_sections`, `parent_index = h1_index` is the index of the preceding H1 within that single file's heading list (markdown.py:183, 195-196). But `import_markdown_notes` accumulates `node_ids` GLOBALLY across all files and does `node_ids[heading.parent_index]` (markdown.py:133-134). When a directory import contains more than one file with headings, every H2's per-file parent index is misapplied to the global list, so prerequisite edges point at a node in a *different* file. Verified end-to-end: importing a.md (`# A1` / `## A2`) and b.md (`# B1` / `## B2`) produces edges `A1->A2` (correct) and `A1->B2` (WRONG — should be `B1->B2`); with three files, n.md's H2s (parent_index=0) all link to a.md's A1. The guard `heading.parent_index < len(node_ids)` (line 133) does not catch this because the global index is in range — it just points at the wrong node. This silently corrupts the imported knowledge graph with bogus prerequisite relationships. The existing test only imports a single file (tests/importers/test_markdown_importer.py:17-30), so it never exercises the cross-file path.
confirmed.evidence: markdown.py:133 `if heading.parent_index is not None and heading.parent_index < len(node_ids):` then `create_knowledge_edge(... source_node_id=node_ids[heading.parent_index] ...)`. parent_index is set per-file at markdown.py:183 (`parent_index = h1_index if level == 2 else None`) where `h1_index = len(headings) - 1` is local to `_parse_heading_sections`.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make parent linkage file-local at creation time. Easiest: in `import_markdown_notes`, track node ids per file (e.g. reset a per-file index base when `heading.file_path` changes, or store `parent_index` as an offset within the current file and add the file's base offset). Alternatively, change `MarkdownHeading` to carry the parent's identity rather than a list index (e.g. resolve the parent heading object), so the edge source is unambiguous regardless of global ordering. Add a multi-file directory test asserting B2 links to B1, not A1.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed real and reproduced end-to-end. In src/lms/importers/markdown.py, `_parse_heading_sections` sets `parent_index = h1_index` (lines 183, 195-196), where `h1_index = len(headings) - 1` is an index into that function's LOCAL per-file `headings` list. But `import_markdown_notes` accumulates `node_ids` GLOBALLY across all files (line 132) and dereferences `node_ids[heading.parent_index]` (line 136). When a directory import contains multiple files with headings, every file's H2 has the same local parent_index (e.g. 0 for the file's first H1), which then indexes the global list and points at the FIRST file's first node.

Reproduced with the real DB session and the actual import_markdown_notes: importing a.md (`# A1` / `## A2`) and b.md (`# B1` / `## B2`) produces persisted KnowledgeEdge rows [('A1','A2'), ('A1','B2')] — the second edge is wrong; it should be ('B1','B2'). With a third file n.md, its H2 also links to A1. This matches the report exactly.

The guard `heading.parent_index < len(node_ids)` (line 133) does NOT catch it: the global index is in range, it just points at the wrong node. create_knowledge_edge (src/lms/graphs/repository.py:236-276) only rejects self-loops (source==target) and cross-scope edges; same-scope wrong edges pass all validation and are written to the DB. So the corruption is silent.

Reachability confirmed: the CLI `import-notes` command accepts "Markdown file or directory" (src/lms/__main__.py:178), and `_markdown_files` (markdown.py:159-165) rglobs and sorts multiple .md files. So this is a first-class, user-facing feature, not dead code. The only existing test (tests/importers/test_markdown_importer.py) imports a single file and never exercises the multi-file path, so coverage genuinely misses it.

I could not refute any element of the claim; the reporter's file/line citations, mechanism, reproduction, and root cause are all accurate.
verdict.notes:
Verified by reproduction against a real in-memory DB session, not just static reading. Persisted edges came out as [('A1','A2'), ('A1','B2')] for a two-file directory import; correct would be [('A1','A2'), ('B1','B2')].

Severity held at high (not bumped to critical) because the corrupted records are explicitly draft-stage candidates intended for human review before promotion: nodes are status="draft"/provenance="imported", and edges are status="draft", confidence=0.5, with notes "Candidate prerequisite inferred from nested Markdown headings." (markdown.py:119-145). This mitigates blast radius somewhat — no authoritative/published graph data is directly written. But it is still silent, error-free cross-file data corruption of a core artifact (the knowledge graph) on a supported CLI feature with zero test coverage, so it clears the bar for high.

Suggested fix is sound: make parent linkage file-local. Cleanest option is to have MarkdownHeading carry the parent's identity (or a stable per-file key) rather than a bare list index, or in import_markdown_notes track a per-file base offset and add it to parent_index. Add a multi-file directory test asserting B2 links to B1, not A1. Note dry-run edge COUNT (line 93) is unaffected — only the source endpoint of created edges is wrong — so the bug is invisible in summaries and only shows up if you inspect edge endpoints.

confirmed.title: Drift scan recomputes a whole-file hash when passage_range is non-line-range, producing false 'stale' results
confirmed.description: `_select_passage` silently returns the ENTIRE file text when `passage_range` does not match `_LINE_RANGE_PATTERN` (repository.py:283-284: `if match is None: return text`). The pattern only accepts numeric line ranges like `L2-L5`/`2-5`. A SourceReference created with passage-level `content` plus a human-readable range (e.g. `"Section 1"`, `"Chapter 2"`, `"pp. 5-10"` — all valid per the 120-char free-text `passage_range` field in schemas.py:21 and models.py:60) stores a hash over the passage content, but the FIRST `scan_source_references` pass recomputes a whole-file hash and flips `drift_status` to `stale` even though nothing changed. Verified: created a markdown-file ref with `passage_range='Section 1'` + `content='Key passage about X.'`; an immediate scan of the UNCHANGED file marked it `stale` (stored passage hash 4fb15be3 vs recomputed whole-file hash 60c961d7). A related variant: `update_source_reference` only recomputes the hash when `content` is supplied (repository.py:138-145), so a PATCH that changes `passage_range` (or `stable_locator`) without re-sending content leaves `content_hash` keyed to the old passage — verified to also yield a false `stale` on the next scan. Both undermine the trustworthiness of the entire drift-detection feature: operators get 'this prompt may be built on stale source material' alerts that are noise.
confirmed.evidence: repository.py:282-284 `match = _LINE_RANGE_PATTERN.match(passage_range.strip())` / `if match is None:` / `return text`. Scan comparison at repository.py:233 `new_status = "current" if new_hash == reference.content_hash else "stale"`.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Do not silently hash the whole file on an unparseable range. Either (a) raise a ValueError in `_select_passage` when `passage_range` is set but unparseable so the inconsistency surfaces at create/scan time, or (b) treat an unparseable range as 'whole file' consistently at BOTH create and scan time. Also, in `update_source_reference`, recompute `content_hash` whenever `passage_range` or `stable_locator` changes for a markdown-file reference (mirror the create-time logic) instead of only when `content` is passed.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed the bug reproduces exactly as described, by running the real code via `uv run`.

Mechanism (verified by reading src/lms/sources/repository.py): At CREATE time, compute_source_hash_for_reference (line 193) returns compute_source_hash(content) when content is supplied, ignoring passage_range entirely. At SCAN time (scan_source_references line 221-228) it calls the same function with content=None, so for markdown-file it reads the file and applies _select_passage. _select_passage (lines 282-284) returns the ENTIRE file text when passage_range does not match _LINE_RANGE_PATTERN (which only accepts numeric line ranges like L2-L5 / 2-5). So the stored passage-content hash never equals the recomputed whole-file hash, and scan_source_references (line 233: `new_status = "current" if new_hash == reference.content_hash else "stale"`) flips an UNCHANGED file to "stale".

Empirical reproduction (markdown-file ref, content='Key passage about X.', passage_range='Section 1'): created drift_status=current with hash 2a471bec; immediate scan of the unchanged file -> stale (recomputed whole-file hash 9dd644dc). Confirmed _select_passage('Section 1') == whole file text, while _select_passage('L2-L2') correctly returns just the line.

Scenario B also confirmed: update_source_reference (lines 138-145) recomputes content_hash only when content is passed. A PATCH changing only passage_range (L2-L2 -> L3-L3) left content_hash keyed to the old passage; next scan of the unchanged file falsely marked it stale.

Reachability through the PUBLIC HTTP API verified: POST /source-references with source_type=markdown-file + free-text passage_range='Section 1' + content returned 201 (no validation rejects free-text ranges); the only constraints on passage_range are max_length=120 (schemas.py:21/34) and String(120) (models.py:60) -- no format/line-range validation anywhere. PATCH with passage_range only returned 200 and left content_hash unchanged. The match-is-None branch (repository.py:284) is uncovered by tests (shown in coverage report); all 17 existing source/importer/api tests pass.

The reporter's code reading, evidence citations, and suggested fixes are all accurate.
verdict.notes:
Real correctness defect, but downgraded high->medium on reachability/impact grounds the reporter understated:

1. Neither first-party producer triggers it. The markdown importer (markdown.py:33-34, 113-114, 182) is the only producer of markdown-file references fed to the drift scan, and it ALWAYS emits passage_range=f"L{start}-L{end}" with content set to exactly the same line slice -- create-hash and scan-hash match, so it is never stale-on-unchanged. The CSV importer (csv_graph.py:83) creates internal-note refs, which scan_source_references skips (repository.py:215). So the bug only fires when an external HTTP/API client supplies a free-text passage_range on a markdown-file ref (scenario A) or PATCHes a range without content (scenario B).

2. Impact is a noisy/incorrect advisory drift_status flag (false 'stale'), not data loss/corruption. It does undermine trust in the drift feature as claimed, and the false-positive direction is 'stale', so alert fatigue from spurious stale flags could mask genuinely stale sources.

Suggested fix is sound: make _select_passage behave consistently at create and scan time (either raise on unparseable-but-set range, or treat unparseable as whole-file at BOTH ends), and have update_source_reference recompute content_hash when passage_range/stable_locator change on a markdown-file ref.

Relevant files: src/lms/sources/repository.py (lines 138-145, 193-202, 221-233, 279-290), src/lms/sources/schemas.py:21/34, src/lms/sources/models.py:60, src/lms/sources/api.py:24-63/94-122.

confirmed.title: Drift detection silently skips all non-markdown source types (tracked as #182), making drift undetectable for most documented types
confirmed.description: `scan_source_references` buckets every non-`markdown-file` reference into a single opaque `skipped` count and continues (repository.py:215-217). The schema enumerates five source types (markdown-file, kindle-highlight, url, pdf-passage, internal-note — models.py:17-23), but a changed `internal-note` or `url` can never surface as `drift_status == "stale"`. `compute_source_hash_for_reference` reinforces this by raising ValueError for every non-file type (repository.py:202). `internal-note` content is actually persisted (the CSV importer stores JSON content via `_row_content`, csv_graph.py:198-199), so it is hashable today with no network — yet it is skipped. The single `skipped` field (DriftScanSummary, repository.py:22-30) also conflates 'deliberately unsupported' (pdf/kindle) with 'no-op', so operators cannot distinguish a coverage gap from nothing-to-do. This is exactly the gap issue #182 documents and asks to close (internal-note hashing, opt-in url fetch, per-reason counts).
confirmed.evidence: repository.py:215 `if reference.source_type != "markdown-file": skipped += 1; continue`; repository.py:202 `raise ValueError("content or content_hash is required for non-file source references")`.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Per #182: branch the scan on `source_type` — keep markdown-file, add an `internal-note` path that re-hashes stored content, and an opt-in `url` path (inject a fetcher, `allow_network=False` default) — and replace the single `skipped` field with explicit per-reason counts (`unsupported_pdf`, `unsupported_kindle`, `skipped`). Note: stored note content is not currently persisted on SourceReference (only the hash is), so the `internal-note` path needs a content column or a way to re-derive content; scope that as part of the fix.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the code. All structural claims are accurate: scan_source_references buckets every non-`markdown-file` reference into a single `skipped` count and continues (src/lms/sources/repository.py:215-217, confirmed verbatim); compute_source_hash_for_reference raises ValueError for all non-file types when no content is passed (repository.py:202); the schema enumerates five source types (models.py:17-23) but DriftScanSummary exposes only one opaque `skipped` field (repository.py:22-30) that conflates deliberately-unsupported (pdf/kindle) with no-op; and tests/sources/test_drift_scan.py covers only markdown, with no internal-note/url coverage. README:233 lists drift-detection as a Milestone-2 capability, and the project tracks this exact gap as OPEN, repo-review-approved issue #182 whose body matches the report. One factual correction (which the reporter themselves flag in their closing note): the description's mid-text claim that internal-note "is hashable today with no network — yet it is skipped" is wrong. The migration alembic/versions/20260525_0004_source_references.py shows source_references has only `content_hash`, no content column; csv_graph.py's `_row_content` (actual path src/lms/importers/csv_graph.py:85,198-199 — the description cited the wrong directory but right file/lines) is hashed at create_source_reference time and the raw content is discarded. Cited line ranges for csv_graph.py used 'src/lms/sources/csv_graph.py' but the file is at 'src/lms/importers/csv_graph.py'; everything else (repository.py, models.py line numbers) is exact.

Behavior is partly intentional/documented (docstring "file-backed"; CLI help "changed markdown file references") and there's an approved tracking issue (#182), so it's a known feature/coverage gap rather than a silent defect in a supported path — no crash, no data corruption, no security impact. confirmed.title: Setext-style Markdown headings crash the importer with an unhandled ValueError
confirmed.description: `_extract_h1_h2_headings` uses Mistune's AST parser, which recognizes setext headings (`Title` underlined with `===` for H1 or `---` for H2 — valid CommonMark). But `_find_heading_line` only matches ATX (`#`-prefixed) headings via `_ATX_HEADING_RE` (markdown.py:17, 259), so it cannot map a setext heading to a source line and raises `ValueError("could not map parsed heading to source line")` (markdown.py:267). Verified: a file with `Big Title\n=========` aborts `plan_markdown_notes` with that ValueError. A single setext heading anywhere in any .md file in the import directory aborts the entire import or dry-run. The `import-notes` CLI path (src/lms/__main__.py:455-478) does not catch this (unlike `import-graph`, which catches CsvGraphImportError), so it surfaces as a raw traceback.
confirmed.evidence: markdown.py:259 `match = _ATX_HEADING_RE.match(lines[line_index])` with `_ATX_HEADING_RE = re.compile(r"^(?P<marks>#{1,6})[ \t]+...")` (line 17); markdown.py:267 `raise ValueError(f"could not map parsed heading to source line: {title!r}")`.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Map heading lines using the Mistune token positions rather than re-scanning for ATX syntax, or extend line-finding to also recognize setext underline patterns (a title line followed by a line of `=`/`-`). At minimum, skip (with a logged warning) headings that cannot be mapped instead of raising, so one setext heading does not abort the whole import. Add a test with setext headings.

verdict.reasoning: All claims verified empirically and in source. (1) Mistune 3.2.1 (the installed version) recognizes setext headings: `md('Big Title\n=========')` emits `{"type": "heading", "style": "setext", "attrs": {"level": 1}, ...}` and `---` yields level 2. (2) `_extract_h1_h2_headings` (markdown.py:206-227) filters only on type=="heading", level in 1..2, and non-empty title — there is NO `style` filter, so setext tokens flow straight to `_find_heading_line`. (3) `_find_heading_line` (markdown.py:251-267) only matches ATX via `_ATX_HEADING_RE` (line 17, `^(?P<marks>#{1,6})[ \t]+...`) and raises `ValueError("could not map parsed heading to source line: ...")` at line 267 when no ATX line matches. (4) Reproduced: `plan_markdown_notes` on a file containing `Big Title\n=========` raises that exact ValueError. (5) Reproduced blast radius: a directory with one good ATX file plus one setext file aborts the ENTIRE import with the ValueError — a single setext heading anywhere in the tree kills the whole run. (6) The `import-notes` CLI branch (__main__.py:455-478) wraps `import_markdown_notes` with no try/except, whereas `import-graph` catches `CsvGraphImportError` (line 488) and `export` catches `ExportImportError` — so this surfaces as a raw, unhandled traceback. (7) No contradicting test coverage: tests/importers/test_markdown_importer.py uses only ATX `#`-prefixed headings; nothing exercises or guards the setext path. I found no upstream guard, validation, or framework handling that refutes the report.
verdict.notes: Severity affirmed at medium. It is a genuine unhandled-crash/robustness bug on valid CommonMark input, and one offending heading aborts an entire import/dry-run with a raw traceback (no CLI catch, unlike the sibling import-graph/export paths). Not higher than medium because it is not a security issue, causes no data corruption, and does not affect the common ATX-only note path — it requires setext-style input to trigger. Suggested fix is sound: prefer mapping via Mistune token positions, or extend line-finding to recognize setext underline patterns; at minimum log-and-skip unmappable headings instead of raising, and add a setext regression test. Confirmed environment: mistune 3.2.1.

confirmed.title: CSV importer: invalid enum values and self-prerequisites pass dry-run but crash mid-import with a raw ValueError
confirmed.description: `_load_rows` validates required columns, duplicate keys, and unknown prerequisite references, but does NOT validate `knowledge_type`, `status`, or `ownership_scope` values, nor reject a row that lists itself as a prerequisite. Those checks happen later inside `create_knowledge_node`/`create_knowledge_edge`, which raise generic `ValueError` (not the importer's `CsvGraphImportError`). Consequences verified: (1) a row with `knowledge_type=not_a_type` passes `dry_run=True` reporting `nodes=2` as if valid, then a real import raises `ValueError: unknown knowledge type 'not_a_type'` partway through after some nodes were already added to the session; (2) a row whose `prerequisites` lists its own title passes `_load_rows` (the self-key IS in `keys`, csv_graph.py:164-168) and dry-run reports `edges=1`, but the real import raises `ValueError: knowledge edge cannot point to its own source node`. Because the CLI `import-graph` handler only catches `CsvGraphImportError` (src/lms/__main__.py:488), these raw ValueErrors escape the clean `CSV graph import failed:` message and surface as tracebacks. The dry-run/real-import divergence breaks the importer's 'validate before writes' contract — a dry-run that reports success does not guarantee the real import succeeds.
confirmed.evidence: csv_graph.py:164-168 builds `keys` including each row's own key, so self-prerequisites pass the unknown-reference check; enum validation is absent in `_load_rows` and only enforced in `create_knowledge_node` (graphs/repository.py:66-68) / `create_knowledge_edge`.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In `_load_rows`, validate `knowledge_type`/`status`/`ownership_scope` against the graphs model enums and reject a row whose prerequisites contain its own `_node_key`, raising `CsvGraphImportError` with the row number — so all failures are caught up front, dry-run matches real-import outcome, and the CLI reports them cleanly.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all claims by reading the code AND reproducing the behavior end-to-end against an in-memory SQLite DB (bypassing conftest, which fails to import due to a missing python-multipart dep — unrelated to this bug).

Confirmed facts:
1. csv_graph.py `_load_rows` (lines 127-175) validates required columns, blank cells, duplicate (scope,title) keys, and unknown prerequisite references — but NOT `knowledge_type`/`status`/`ownership_scope` enum values, and does NOT reject self-prerequisites.
2. Line 164 builds `keys` from each row's own (scope,title), so a row listing its own title as a prerequisite passes the unknown-reference check at line 168 (confirmed in repro: dry-run reports edges=1).
3. Enum validation lives only in create_knowledge_node (repository.py:65-68) and the self-loop guard in create_knowledge_edge (repository.py:266-267), both raising plain `ValueError`, not `CsvGraphImportError`.
4. CLI handler __main__.py:480-489 catches ONLY `CsvGraphImportError`; there is no outer try/except around command dispatch, so a raw ValueError escapes as a traceback rather than the clean "CSV graph import failed:" message.

Reproduction results (fresh session per case):
- knowledge_type=not_a_type: dry-run nodes=2 (reported valid); real import raised `ValueError: unknown knowledge type 'not_a_type'` with 1 node already flushed to the session; isinstance CsvGraphImportError = False.
- status=bogus_status: dry-run OK; raw ValueError.
- ownership_scope=galaxy: dry-run OK; raw ValueError.
- self-prerequisite: dry-run edges=1; real import raised `ValueError: knowledge edge cannot point to its own source node`; isinstance CsvGraphImportError = False.

No test covers these cases — test_unknown_prerequisite_fails_before_writes only covers a reference to a different non-existent node. The dry-run/real-import divergence and the CLI traceback-escape are both real. Note: session_scope() (db/session.py:42-45) rolls back on exception, so no partial data is committed to the DB — the practical impact is a misleading dry-run plus an ugly traceback instead of a clean error message, not data corruption.
verdict.notes: Severity kept at medium (not downgraded): the codebase explicitly enshrines the "validate before writes" / "dry-run reports counts without writes" invariant via dedicated tests (test_unknown_prerequisite_fails_before_writes, test_dry_run_reports_counts_without_writes), so this is a genuine contract violation, not a cosmetic nit. Mitigating factor that keeps it from being high: session_scope rolls back on exception (atomic — no partial commit / DB corruption), and import-graph is an admin/bootstrap CLI surface, not a user-facing hot path. A reviewer could reasonably argue low given the atomic rollback, but the broken documented invariant + clean CLI-error escape justify medium. Suggested fix (validate knowledge_type/status/ownership_scope against the graphs model enums and reject self-prerequisites in _load_rows, raising CsvGraphImportError with the row number) is correct and well-scoped. Relevant files: /Users/teacher/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/Learning/Code/learning-management-system/src/lms/importers/csv_graph.py (lines 127-175 for _load_rows, 164-168 for the self-key gap), /Users/teacher/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/Learning/Code/learning-management-system/src/lms/graphs/repository.py (66-68 enum checks, 266-267 self-loop guard), /Users/teacher/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/Learning/Code/learning-management-system/src/lms/__main__.py (480-489 CLI handler), /Users/teacher/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/Learning/Code/learning-management-system/src/lms/graphs/models.py (enum tuples), /Users/teacher/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/Learning/Code/learning-management-system/src/lms/db/session.py (42-45 rollback-on-exception).

confirmed.title: Both importers crash on non-UTF-8 input with an uncaught UnicodeDecodeError
confirmed.description: `import_csv_graph` opens the file with `encoding="utf-8"` (csv_graph.py:67) and the markdown importer reads with `read_text(encoding="utf-8")` (markdown.py:169, also repository.py:275 in the drift path). A file in latin-1/cp1252 (common for Excel/Windows CSV exports and notes) raises `UnicodeDecodeError`, which is not wrapped. Verified: a latin-1 CSV with `Café` and a latin-1 markdown file both crash with `UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xe9`. For the CSV path the CLI only catches `CsvGraphImportError` (src/lms/__main__.py:488), so the user gets a traceback rather than the documented 'CSV graph import failed' message, despite `CsvGraphImportError` existing precisely for 'cannot be imported safely'.
confirmed.evidence: csv_graph.py:67 `with path.open(newline="", encoding="utf-8") as csv_file:`; markdown.py:169 `text = file_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")`.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap the file read in each importer and translate `UnicodeDecodeError` into `CsvGraphImportError` (CSV) / a caught error or skip-with-warning (markdown), naming the file and suggesting UTF-8 re-encoding. Optionally accept an explicit `encoding` argument. Keep CLI behavior consistent so encoding failures produce the clean error message, not a traceback.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified and reproduced. Both importers read with UTF-8 and do not wrap UnicodeDecodeError.

CONFIRMED claims:
- csv_graph.py:67 `with path.open(newline="", encoding="utf-8")` — confirmed.
- markdown.py:169 `text = file_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")` — confirmed.
- A latin-1 CSV containing `Café` and a latin-1 markdown file both crash with `UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xe9` — reproduced directly via import_csv_graph(...) and import_markdown_notes(...).
- CLI `import-graph` (__main__.py:480-489) catches only CsvGraphImportError, so the UnicodeDecodeError escapes as a raw traceback rather than the documented "CSV graph import failed: ..." message — reproduced end-to-end through the real lms.__main__.main entrypoint (traceback terminates in csv.py fieldnames -> codecs decode). There is no outer catch-all around main(), and `import-notes` (lines 455-478) has no try/except at all, so it would also traceback.
- CsvGraphImportError's docstring is explicitly "Raised when a CSV graph file cannot be imported safely," so an encoding failure logically belongs inside that handling — the reporter's framing is sound.
- No test exercises non-UTF-8 input (all importer test fixtures are written encoding="utf-8"), and no doc/CLI help declares a UTF-8-only input constraint, so this is not documented intended behavior.

MINOR INACCURACY (does not undermine the finding): the report cites "repository.py:275 in the drift path" implying a read_text there. In src/lms/graphs/repository.py:275 that line is actually `session.get(...)`. The real `path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")` lives at src/lms/sources/repository.py:275 (same line number, different module). So the drift-path read does exist; the citation just under-qualified the module path.
verdict.notes: Severity stays low (reporter agreed): real, reproducible crash on common cp1252/latin-1 Excel/Windows exports, but it is operator-facing CLI import tooling, fail-safe (import aborts cleanly with no data corruption), and the only harm is a raw traceback instead of a clean error message. Suggested fix is appropriate: catch UnicodeDecodeError in import_csv_graph and re-raise as CsvGraphImportError naming the file; for markdown, either translate to a caught error or skip-with-warning; optionally accept an explicit encoding argument. Note the markdown CLI path (import-notes) has no try/except at all, so wrapping the read isn't sufficient there unless the CLI also handles the resulting error. Citation nit: the drift-path read_text is at src/lms/sources/repository.py:275, not graphs/repository.py:275.

confirmed.title: Operator-configured daily cost cap (LLM_DAILY_CAP_MICRO_USD) is silently ignored; production client hardcodes 1_000_000
confirmed.description: _default_client() loads the env-driven cap into LLMConfig.global_daily_cap_micro_usd (api.py:188, from load_llm_config_from_env which reads LLM_DAILY_CAP_MICRO_USD, config.py:74-75), but the object that actually enforces the kill-switch is DailyBudgetTracker, and it is constructed with a hardcoded global_cap_micro_usd=1_000_000 (api.py:197). budget.preflight() only ever checks DailyBudgetTracker.global_cap_micro_usd (budgets.py:36); LLMConfig.global_daily_cap_micro_usd is never read for enforcement anywhere (confirmed via grep). So an operator who sets LLM_DAILY_CAP_MICRO_USD to lower spend gets no effect: the real cap stays at the hardcoded 1_000_000. Likewise per-mode caps are always empty (mode_caps_micro_usd={}), so per-mode budgeting never fires either. This defeats the project's documented fail-closed cost kill-switch for the deployed client.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In _default_client(), pass the env-derived cap into the tracker: DailyBudgetTracker(mode_caps_micro_usd=env_config.per_mode_daily_cap_micro_usd, global_cap_micro_usd=env_config.global_daily_cap_micro_usd). Remove the hardcoded 1_000_000. Optionally drop the now-redundant global_daily_cap_micro_usd field from LLMConfig or wire LLMClient to read it, so there is a single source of truth for the cap.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified the bug is real by reading the cited files and running the code. In _default_client() (src/lms/llm/api.py:183-199), the env-driven cap from LLM_DAILY_CAP_MICRO_USD is loaded into LLMConfig.global_daily_cap_micro_usd (api.py:188, via load_llm_config_from_env at config.py:74-75), but the DailyBudgetTracker — the object that actually enforces the kill-switch — is constructed with a hardcoded global_cap_micro_usd=1_000_000 and mode_caps_micro_usd={} (api.py:196-197). Enforcement happens only in DailyBudgetTracker.preflight() (budgets.py:36, checks self.global_cap_micro_usd), called from LLMClient.complete() via self.budget.preflight() (client.py:116). A grep across src/ confirms LLMConfig.global_daily_cap_micro_usd is never read for enforcement anywhere — its only read site is api.py:188, which merely copies it into another config object that no enforcement code consults.

Confirmed empirically: setting LLM_DAILY_CAP_MICRO_USD=5000 yielded config.global_daily_cap_micro_usd=5000 but the enforced budget.global_cap_micro_usd stayed at 1000000 (200x higher) — the operator override is silently ignored.

The path can spend real money: authoring_assist_propose_route (api.py:331-373) calls propose_authoring_drafts without provider_name, which resolves to config.default_provider. build_default_providers (providers.py:218-239) sets default_provider="anthropic" whenever an API key is present (no package-import check at registration), so with a key set this route issues real billed Anthropic calls bounded only by the hardcoded cap. The same hardcoded-1_000_000 wiring defect also appears in src/lms/__main__.py:377/381 and 438/442. The existing test (tests/llm/test_client_routing.py:106) only asserts the config layer captures the env value; no test wires that value into the tracker for the _default_client() path, so the bug is uncovered.

The reporter's technical claims are all accurate.
verdict.notes: Downgraded from high to medium. It is a genuine fail-open cost-control defect that defeats the documented "hard kill-switch with low default" intent (docs/product/project-plan.md:408, early-design-decisions.md:511) on a path that can issue real paid Anthropic calls, and operator LLM_DAILY_CAP_MICRO_USD overrides plus per-mode caps are silently ignored. But the blast radius is bounded: the kill-switch is not disabled, just stuck at 1_000_000 micro-USD = 1.00 USD/day (vs the 0.20 USD config default), the tracker is process-local and resets on restart, and the primary learner /sessions route hardcodes provider_name="fake". The reporter's "data-integrity" category is a mild mischaracterization — this is cost-control/config-enforcement, not data corruption. Worst realistic outcome is modestly higher-than-intended spend capped near ~1 USD per process lifetime, not unbounded spend. Suggested fix is correct: pass env_config.global_daily_cap_micro_usd and env_config.per_mode_daily_cap_micro_usd into DailyBudgetTracker in _default_client() (and the two __main__.py sites), and remove the hardcoded 1_000_000.

confirmed.title: No error handling or retry around the real Anthropic call; transient/rate-limit/timeout errors surface as unhandled 500s
confirmed.description: AnthropicProvider.complete() calls client.messages.create(**call_kwargs) (providers.py:182) with no try/except, no retry, and no backoff (confirmed: no retry/tenacity/backoff/APIError/RateLimit handling anywhere in src/lms/llm). Any anthropic.APIError, RateLimitError (429), APITimeoutError, or connection error propagates raw out of the wrapper as a non-LLMError exception. The only real-provider call site, authoring_assist_propose_route (api.py:331-359), catches only (SourceConstraintViolation, ValueError), so a provider/network failure becomes an uncaught exception -> HTTP 500 with a stack trace, rather than a controlled 502/503. There is also no automatic retry for transient 429/5xx, so normal rate limiting fails the user-facing request outright.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap the create() call in AnthropicProvider.complete() in try/except for anthropic SDK errors and re-raise as a new LLMError subclass (e.g. ProviderCallError) so callers can catch a single wrapper exception type. Add bounded retry with exponential backoff for RateLimitError/APITimeoutError/5xx (either via the SDK's max_retries on the Anthropic client or an explicit loop). Update authoring_assist_propose_route to catch the new wrapper error and return 502/503.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the cited code and tracing the full call chain. providers.py:182 calls client.messages.create(**call_kwargs) with no try/except (verified lines 164-215). rg across all of src/ found zero retry/tenacity/backoff/RateLimitError/APIError/APITimeoutError handling, and AnthropicProvider._build_client() (lines 150-157) constructs Anthropic(api_key=...) without setting max_retries, so the adapter adds no resilience of its own. The wrapper (client.py) only raises LLMError subclasses for mode/trace/provider-lookup/structured-output/source-constraint failures and has no try/except around provider.complete() at client.py:118, so a raw anthropic SDK exception propagates unchanged. The sole real-provider route, authoring_assist_propose_route (api.py:331-359), catches only (SourceConstraintViolation, ValueError); the other LLM route (api.py:518) pins provider_name='fake'. main.py create_app installs no add_exception_handler/exception middleware, so an uncaught anthropic exception yields FastAPI's default HTTP 500 with a server-side traceback rather than a controlled 502/503. anthropic>=0.40.0 is a declared production dependency (pyproject.toml) and _default_client() (api.py:169-179) selects the real AnthropicProvider as default whenever a key is configured, so this path is live, not dead code. No tests assert any error-wrapping/retry/502/503 behavior. The only soft spot in the report is the retry claim: the Anthropic SDK retries some 429/5xx by default, but it still raises after exhaustion and does not cover connection/timeout errors, so the core defect (raw 500 / no wrapper exception type / no controlled status) holds.
verdict.notes: Downgraded from high to medium. Blast radius is a single authoring/reviewer-facing draft-creation endpoint (/authoring-assist/propose); the learner-facing study-coach route is pinned to the fake provider. The failure mode is a degraded 500 + server-side stack trace and no transient-error resilience on rate limits/timeouts — a genuine error-handling/operability gap. It does not corrupt data (drafts persist only after a successful response; session.commit runs after the call returns), does not leak secrets beyond a traceback, and does not break a primary user journey, so it is not high severity. Suggested fix is sound: wrap create() in a ProviderCallError(LLMError) subclass, add bounded exponential backoff for RateLimitError/APITimeoutError/5xx (or set Anthropic(max_retries=...)), and have the route catch the wrapper error to return 502/503.

confirmed.title: DailyBudgetTracker is mutated without locking under FastAPI's sync-route threadpool; concurrent calls can overspend the cap
confirmed.description: All LLM routes are sync `def` (api.py:331,493, etc.), so FastAPI executes them in a threadpool. _default_client() is @lru_cache(maxsize=1) (api.py:168), so every concurrent request shares one DailyBudgetTracker instance. preflight() reads self._global_spend and record() does self._global_spend += cost (budgets.py:35-36,59) with no lock. This is a non-atomic read-modify-write across threads: two requests can both pass preflight against the same pre-call _global_spend and then both record(), pushing actual spend past global_cap_micro_usd. The kill-switch's entire purpose (fail-closed before money is spent) is undermined precisely when traffic is high. Lost updates on _mode_spend/_global_spend can also under-count recorded spend.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Guard preflight+record with a threading.Lock held across the read-and-update (or make preflight reserve the projected cost atomically and reconcile in record). Simplest: add a threading.Lock to DailyBudgetTracker and acquire it in preflight() and record(); ideally expose a single reserve/commit API so the check and the spend update are one critical section.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed the mechanism against the code. The cited file is actually src/lms/llm/api.py (line numbers map there); src/lms/api.py does not exist, but the reporter's references are otherwise accurate.

1. Sync routes -> real thread parallelism. authoring_assist_propose_route (api.py:331) and create_llm_session_route (api.py:493) are plain `def`. The app is deployed via `uvicorn lms.main:app` (render.yaml:23, Dockerfile:87, docker-compose.yml:50). Starlette/FastAPI dispatches sync handlers to an anyio worker threadpool, so concurrent requests run on distinct OS threads with preemptive scheduling — not a single-threaded async loop. The race is therefore physically possible.

2. Shared singleton. _default_client() is @lru_cache(maxsize=1) (api.py:168), so every request shares one LLMClient -> one DailyBudgetTracker.

3. Non-atomic check-then-act with a wide window. LLMClient.complete() calls self.budget.preflight() (client.py:116), then the provider network call, then self.budget.record() (client.py:125). preflight reads self._global_spend (budgets.py:35) and record does self._global_spend += cost (budgets.py:59). No lock exists in budgets.py, client.py, or the call sites (grep for Lock/threading returned nothing in src/lms/llm). Multiple threads can pass preflight against the same stale _global_spend during the provider-call gap and then all record, overshooting global_cap_micro_usd; concurrent += can also lose updates and under-count spend. This is a genuine TOCTOU / lost-update bug, correctly diagnosed.

The suggested fix (a threading.Lock around a combined reserve/commit critical section) is appropriate.
verdict.notes: Downgraded from high to medium because the reporter overstated blast radius and missed design context. (a) Only one route can actually spend real money: the study-coach route create_llm_session_route hardcodes provider_name="fake" (api.py:530,541), so it never bills regardless of the race; only authoring_assist_propose_route reaches the config-derived (potentially real Anthropic) provider. The "all LLM routes" framing is too broad. (b) budgets.py:1-6 documents the tracker as an intentionally process-local, best-effort kill-switch ("not ... cross-process spend reconciliation"), and the default global cap is just 0.20 USD/day (config.py:28), bounding worst-case overshoot to a small multiple of one call's cost. Any multi-worker/multi-replica deploy already defeats the global cap independent of this race. Still a real in-process correctness defect involving real money on one route, so not a non-issue — medium is the right level. No concurrency test exists for this (tests/llm/test_client_budget.py covers single-threaded behavior only).

confirmed.title: Budget preflight under-estimates output cost when max_tokens is None (assumes 64, provider allows 1024), so one call can exceed the cap
confirmed.description: _estimate_cost() computes output_tokens = max_tokens if set else max(64, input_tokens) (client.py:268). authoring_assist.propose_authoring_drafts() calls client.complete() with no max_tokens (authoring_assist.py:104-112), so preflight assumes ~64 output tokens. But AnthropicProvider.complete() sends max_tokens = default_max_tokens = 1024 when the caller passes None (providers.py:147,177). The provider can therefore return up to 1024 output tokens whose actual cost is booked by budget.record() after the call. A call that preflight projects as well under the cap can actually cost up to ~16x its projected output portion, breaching the 'fail-closed before money is spent' guarantee from budgets.py:1-6 on a single request.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the preflight ceiling match what the provider will actually request: when max_tokens is None, estimate using the provider's default_max_tokens (e.g. getattr(provider, 'default_max_tokens', 1024)) rather than max(64, input_tokens). Alternatively, have LLMClient.complete() resolve an explicit max_tokens before both preflight and the provider call so the estimate and the request use the same bound.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at the code level and the core mechanism is real. _estimate_cost (src/lms/llm/client.py:268) assumes output_tokens = max(64, input_tokens) when max_tokens is None, while AnthropicProvider.complete (src/lms/llm/providers.py:177) substitutes default_max_tokens=1024 (providers.py:147) for a None max_tokens. authoring_assist.propose_authoring_drafts (src/lms/llm/authoring_assist.py:104-112) calls complete() with no max_tokens, and the production API client (src/lms/llm/api.py:175-199) registers the real AnthropicProvider when a key is set, so this path is live. budget.record() (client.py:125) books actual cost after the call, so the preflight under-estimates the output portion ~16x (1024/64) — net ~14x for Sonnet, ~71x for Opus. I confirmed the numbers: a typical authoring-assist prompt preflights at ~1,083 micro-USD but the same call can actually cost ~15,510 (Sonnet) or ~77,550 (Opus) micro-USD. The design intent that this violates is documented by test_unpriced_provider_uses_fail_closed_preflight_estimate (tests/llm/test_client_budget.py:102-124), which establishes the preflight is meant to be a conservative fail-closed bound. No existing test guards this gap.

However, the reporter overstates the impact in two ways. (1) The title's claim that "one call can exceed the cap" is false under the shipped config: the production global cap is 1,000,000 micro-USD / 1 USD/day (hardcoded in api.py:197 and __main__.py:381,442), and a single worst-case call is at most ~5,170 (Haiku, the production default), ~15,510 (Sonnet), or ~77,550 (Opus) micro-USD — none individually breach the cap. (2) The cap can only be breached via ACCUMULATED under-estimation, and because preflight checks actual running spend (_global_spend from record()) plus the low projection, the worst-case overshoot of the daily kill-switch is bounded by one call's actual-minus-projected delta: ~14,427 micro-USD (~$0.014) for Sonnet, ~$0.077 for Opus. So the "fail-closed before money is spent" guarantee is genuinely weakened at the margin, but the practical exposure is a cents-level overshoot of a daily cap, not an unbounded or single-call breach. The suggested fix (use getattr(provider,'default_max_tokens',1024) in the preflight, or resolve max_tokens once before both preflight and the provider call) is correct and appropriate.
verdict.notes: Real defect, severity downgraded medium->low. The under-estimate (~14x Sonnet / ~71x Opus on the output portion) is accurate, but the headline "one call can exceed the cap" is false under the production 1,000,000 micro-USD/day cap: worst single call ~77,550 micro-USD (Opus). The fail-closed guarantee is breached only via accumulated under-estimation, with a bounded overshoot of ~$0.014 (Sonnet) to ~$0.077 (Opus) past the daily cap. The FakeProvider also lacks these exact attr names (it has input_token_cost_micro_usd=1/output=4), so it happens to be near-scale, but the 1000/4000 defaults are inconsistent with the rest of the module's accounting unit.

verdict.reasoning:
The report's factual claims all check out. _estimate_cost falls back to input_rate=1_000 / output_rate=4_000 micro-USD per token (src/lms/llm/client.py:269-270), while the module's real accounting scale is far smaller: the Anthropic price table feeds _anthropic_cost_micro_usd, where the USD-per-Mtoken values (e.g. Sonnet (3.00, 15.00)) are used directly as micro-USD-per-token (providers.py:122-127), confirmed by tests/llm/test_anthropic_provider.py:177-180 (1000*3 + 500*15 = 10500). FakeProvider uses 1/4 (providers.py:60-61). So the fallback is ~250-300x the real per-token scale (I measured ~267x for a short prompt: 258_000 vs 966 micro-USD, i.e. 26% of the default $1.00 cap for a single 2-word call). The fallback constants are genuinely in a different unit scale than everything else in the module — a real internal-consistency defect and a foot-gun for the OpenAI/Bedrock adapters explicitly named as future work in providers.py:5-6.

However, the severity is correctly capped at low, and arguably it is closer to a latent code-quality issue than an active bug:
1. No production path can hit the fallback today. Every provider is registered via build_default_providers, which only ever creates FakeProvider (has 1/4 attrs) or AnthropicProvider (defines input/output_token_cost_micro_usd = 3/15 at providers.py:142-143, asserted by test_anthropic_provider.py:241-242). The reporter correctly acknowledges this.
2. The over-estimate is in the fail-closed direction, which is the explicitly documented intended budget posture: budgets.py:4 ("hard kill-switch posture ... fail-closed preflight") and docs/product/early-design-decisions.md:511 ("Better to fail-closed early than to discover an unexpected month-end bill"). The _estimate_cost docstring itself says "Conservative preflight cost estimate." For a kill-switch, over-estimation is the safe error direction; an under-estimate would be the dangerous bug. test_unpriced_provider_uses_fail_closed_preflight_estimate (test_client_budget.py:102-124) treats the unpriced-fallback BudgetExceeded as intended behavior.
3. The spurious-rejection harm requires a speculative future adapter that omits the attrs and ignores the existing AnthropicProvider pattern.

Corroborating the confusion the inconsistency causes: tests/llm/test_client_routing.py:200-201 carries a stale, factually-wrong comment claiming "AnthropicProvider has no preflight-rate attrs so _estimate_cost falls back to the 1000/4000 micro-USD heuristic" — but AnthropicProvider does define them, so that path actually uses 3/15. That stale comment is evidence the mismatched scale already misled a contributor, which strengthens the case that this is worth aligning (low priority). Note for any fix: it should preserve conservatism — naively defaulting to (3, 15) would under-estimate Opus (15/75), so deriving a conservative bound from the price table is safer than hard-coding Sonnet's rates.
verdict.notes: Reporter's facts and ~300x magnitude are accurate; production paths (Fake 1/4, Anthropic 3/15) are unaffected. Real but minor: a unit-scale inconsistency / future-adapter foot-gun rather than an active correctness defect, and the over-estimate direction matches the intended fail-closed kill-switch posture. confirmed.title: get_session commits on every request including read-only GETs and the failed-login path; write routes then double-commit
confirmed.description: get_session wraps session_scope(), which unconditionally calls session.commit() when the generator resumes without an exception. I verified empirically that a plain GET /audit/events triggers exactly one COMMIT. Consequences: (1) Every read endpoint opens a read/write transaction and commits it, taking write-path overhead and holding locks longer than a read-only transaction would. (2) Routes that signal failure by RETURNING a non-2xx response instead of raising — e.g. login_submit (src/lms/auth/login.py:141-148) returns HTMLResponse(status_code=401) on bad credentials — do not raise, so session_scope still commits; harmless today but fragile if such a handler ever leaves partial writes. (3) All write routes (sources/api.py:56, evidence/api.py:30, llm/api.py:360/467/573, scheduling/api.py:113, cases/api.py, capability/api.py:72) call session.commit() themselves and then session_scope commits a second time on teardown. The second commit is a harmless no-op today but means transaction ownership is split between the route and the dependency, and any exception raised AFTER the in-route commit (e.g. session.refresh failing) would be handled by session_scope's rollback on an already-committed transaction.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make get_session a read-by-default dependency that does NOT auto-commit: yield the session and only rollback+close in teardown, leaving commit to the write routes that already call it explicitly. (Keep session_scope's commit-on-exit for scripts/services.) This removes the double-commit, stops read endpoints from committing, and makes transaction ownership explicit. Update the handful of write routes to ensure each commits exactly once (they already do).
confirmed.evidence: Live check: 'GET /audit/events status 200 commits during read: 1'. session.py:40-45 'yield session; session.commit()'. sources/api.py:56 'session.commit()' inside route while the same session is later committed again by session_scope.
confirmed.cluster: Platform & API surface (src/lms/api, src/lms/db, alembic, src/lms/audit)

verdict.reasoning:
The factual core of the report is accurate and I reproduced both empirical claims live against the real (non-overridden) get_session.

CONFIRMED:
1. get_session (src/lms/db/session.py:50-53) wraps session_scope, which unconditionally calls session.commit() on clean generator resume (line 42).
2. A read-only GET /audit/events triggers exactly 1 COMMIT and 1 transaction-begin. The audit route (src/lms/api/audit.py:17-31) injects get_session directly with no auth dependency, so the commit comes purely from session_scope wrapping a SELECT. Reproduced: "commits during read: 1".
3. Write routes double-commit: POST /source-references produced exactly 2 commits (in-route session.commit() at src/lms/sources/api.py:56 plus session_scope teardown commit). Reproduced: "commits during write: 2". The pattern holds across the cited write routes.
4. Failed-login path: authenticate() (src/lms/auth/repository.py:48-52) returns None after only a SELECT, so session_scope commits an empty transaction. Genuinely harmless, as the reporter concedes.

WHERE THE REPORT OVERSTATES OR IS INCOMPLETE:
- The data-integrity framing is the weakest part. I tested the headline worst case ("exception after in-route commit -> session_scope rollback on already-committed transaction"): the rollback is a no-op on already-committed data; the row stays durable. No data loss or corruption. So no actual data-integrity failure mode exists; the real concern is transaction hygiene / minor read-path overhead. The reporter hedges every consequence ("harmless today", "harmless no-op", "fragile if").
- The claim "all write routes call session.commit() themselves" is incomplete and partly wrong. The auth write paths deliberately do NOT: login_submit never commits, and both the successful-login password-rehash upgrade (repository.py:54-56) and get_or_create_local_dev_user (repository.py:80-97, invoked on nearly every authenticated request when auth_required=False) persist via flush-only and rely entirely on session_scope's commit-on-exit. The suggested fix ("leave commit to the write routes that already call it explicitly") would silently drop these writes unless those paths were also patched — a real risk the reporter did not identify, so the fix is more invasive than presented.

Conclusion: real as a design/transaction-hygiene wart, but benign today with no demonstrated correctness or data-integrity failure.
verdict.notes: Reproduced both claims empirically by patching the lru_cached get_engine to an in-memory SQLite engine and counting SQLAlchemy commit/begin events with the REAL get_session (the repo's own test fixtures override get_session with a no-commit version, so the production commit behavior is only exercised outside the suite). Downgraded medium->low: category is performance/maintainability, not data-integrity (rollback-after-commit is a no-op, no data loss). Caveat for any fix: the suggested 'reads-no-commit, writes-own-commit' change must also update the flush-only auth write paths (password rehash on successful login, and get_or_create_local_dev_user) or those writes will be silently dropped.

confirmed.evidence: main.py lines 83-103 include 20+ routers with no dependency; `rg require_authenticated_user src/` returns only comments in main.py and the def/docstring in login.py — no usage. Every router: `APIRouter(tags=[...])` with no dependencies=. render.yaml:33 `value: "true"` for AUTH_REQUIRED.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Enforce the dependency app-wide. Simplest correct fix: in create_app(), add a router-level dependency on each protected include, e.g. define `auth_dep = [Depends(require_authenticated_user)]` and pass `app.include_router(attempts_router, dependencies=auth_dep)` (and same for inspect, audit, sources, graphs, prompts, feedback, cases, competencies, capability, mastery, review_queue, llm, and all ui routers), leaving login_router and health_router unguarded. Alternatively register a single global dependency via `FastAPI(dependencies=[Depends(require_authenticated_user)])` and exempt /login + /health inside the dependency. Add an integration test that runs the REAL create_app() with AUTH_REQUIRED=true and asserts an unauthenticated GET /inspect/... and GET /audit/events return 401/302, instead of the current test that builds a private app.

verdict.reasoning:
Every claim in the report is verified at the code level AND proven empirically.

CODE EVIDENCE:
- `rg require_authenticated_user` across the repo returns it ONLY in: main.py:59,75 (comments), login.py (its own def/docstring/__all__), docs/architecture/auth.md (docs), and tests/auth/test_authenticated_session.py. It is attached to ZERO production routes. The dependency is never even imported into any src/lms module outside login.py itself (main.py imports only `router as login_router`, not the dependency).
- main.py create_app() (lines 83-103) mounts inspect, audit, sources, graphs, prompts, evidence/attempts, feedback, cases, competencies, capability, mastery, scheduling, llm, and all 7 UI routers via plain `app.include_router(...)` with NO `dependencies=` arg.
- FastAPI(...) at main.py:46 takes only title/description/version — no global `dependencies=[...]`.
- Every APIRouter is built as `APIRouter(tags=[...])` / `APIRouter(prefix=..., tags=...)` with no dependencies= (confirmed across all 23 router constructions). inspect.py:26 (/inspect/learners/{id}/overview) and audit.py:17 (/audit/events) carry only `Depends(get_session)`.
- The ONLY middleware is SessionMiddleware (signs cookies, does not require them). No BaseHTTPMiddleware, no auth middleware.
- render.yaml sets AUTH_REQUIRED="true" and ENABLE_LOCAL_IDENTITY_ROUTES="false" for the deployed Render service; startCommand runs uvicorn directly with no edge/proxy auth (no nginx/oauth2-proxy/traefik/basic-auth/Cloudflare Access anywhere in render.yaml, docker-compose, Dockerfile, or deployment docs).
- tests/auth/test_authenticated_session.py:48-101 explicitly builds its OWN throwaway FastAPI() (the fixture docstring says "rather than instantiating the full create_app() factory") and manually attaches Depends(require_authenticated_user) to /protected-html and /protected-api. The 6 passing tests therefore give false confidence — they never exercise the real app.

EMPIRICAL PROOF:
I ran the REAL create_app() with Settings(auth_required=True) (exactly production config), no login and no session cookie, and got:
  ANON GET /inspect/learners/learner-123/overview -> HTTP 200 (returns learner_id, mastery, recent_evidence, prompt_provenance, scheduler...)
  ANON GET /audit/events -> HTTP 200 (returns the audit event list)
Expected behavior per require_authenticated_user's docstring would be 401 (JSON) or 302 redirect. The gate is completely bypassed.

ONE NUANCE (does not reduce severity): in production ENABLE_LOCAL_IDENTITY_ROUTES=false, so the auth_router and learners_router (the only conditionally-mounted routers, lines 79-81) are NOT mounted. But that is irrelevant — the inspect/audit/evidence/mastery/llm/UI routers are mounted unconditionally and ARE reachable anonymously, as demonstrated. The login form protects nothing and AUTH_REQUIRED=true is inert.
verdict.notes: Confirmed real and critical. Files: src/lms/main.py (create_app lines 77-106 — every include_router lacks dependencies=, FastAPI() at line 46 lacks global dependencies=); src/lms/auth/login.py:172 (require_authenticated_user — defined, documented, never wired); src/lms/api/inspect.py:22-26 and src/lms/api/audit.py:14-17 (protected data endpoints with only Depends(get_session)); render.yaml:32-39 (AUTH_REQUIRED="true", ENABLE_LOCAL_IDENTITY_ROUTES="false", no edge auth); tests/auth/test_authenticated_session.py:48-101 (builds throwaway app — 6 tests pass but never touch the real factory, hence false confidence). Empirically reproduced: anonymous GET /inspect/learners/{id}/overview and GET /audit/events both return HTTP 200 against the real create_app() with auth_required=True. Suggested fix in the report is sound: either pass dependencies=[Depends(require_authenticated_user)] on each protected include_router (leaving login_router + health_router open) or set FastAPI(dependencies=[...]) globally with /login and /health exempted, plus an integration test that runs the REAL create_app() with AUTH_REQUIRED=true and asserts 401/302 for anonymous protected requests.

confirmed.title: Username-enumeration timing side-channel in authenticate()
confirmed.description: authenticate() returns None immediately when get_user_by_username finds no row, so the expensive Argon2id verify only runs for usernames that exist. The response-time difference (a DB lookup vs a DB lookup + ~tens of ms of Argon2 hashing) lets an attacker enumerate valid usernames despite the deliberately generic 'Invalid username or password.' message at login.py:145. The code comment at login.py:136-137 explicitly states the goal is to avoid username enumeration, but the timing channel defeats it.
confirmed.evidence: repository.py:49 `if user is None: return None` runs before any hashing; verify_password (passwords.py:60) only executes for existing users. Argon2 default cost is ~tens of ms, easily distinguishable over the network.
confirmed.suggested_fix: When the user is not found, perform a dummy Argon2 verify against a fixed throwaway hash before returning None, so both branches cost the same. E.g. compute a module-level `_DUMMY_HASH = PasswordHasher().hash("x")` once and call `verify_password(_DUMMY_HASH, password)` (discarding the result) in the user-is-None branch of authenticate(). Small, localized change in repository.py/passwords.py.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed real after reading the cited code, the surrounding login flow, the deploy config, and an empirical timing measurement. authenticate() (src/lms/auth/repository.py:48-50) does `user = get_user_by_username(...)` then `if user is None: return None`, returning before any password hashing. Only for an existing user does it call verify_password (src/lms/auth/passwords.py:60 -> argon2 _HASHER.verify). I measured the delta with the project's argon2 defaults (time_cost=3, memory_cost=64 MiB, parallelism=4): the existing-user verify branch takes ~30 ms vs ~0.0004 ms for the no-user branch. The DB lookup is common to both branches and cancels as common-mode, so the ~30 ms Argon2 cost is a clean, network-distinguishable timing oracle that lets an attacker enumerate valid usernames — directly defeating the stated anti-enumeration intent at src/lms/auth/login.py:136-137 and the generic 'Invalid username or password.' message at login.py:145.

I tried to refute it and could not. The path is production-reachable, not a local-dev artifact: render.yaml sets AUTH_REQUIRED='true' (and docs/development/deployment.md:37 confirms it), so /login -> authenticate() is the live deployed auth flow. There is no rate limiting anywhere in the repo to blunt the oracle, and the simpler user-disclosure routes (create_local_user via auth_router/learners_router) are gated behind enable_local_identity_routes, which render.yaml sets to 'false' — so in production there is no easier enumeration vector that would moot the timing channel. Every factual claim in the report is accurate, including the suggested fix (a dummy Argon2 verify against a fixed throwaway hash in the user-is-None branch), which is the standard CWE-208/CWE-203 mitigation.

Severity stays medium (not inflated, not under-rated): this is an information-disclosure/username-enumeration weakness, not an auth bypass or secret leak — it reveals which usernames exist but does not by itself grant access.
verdict.notes: Files: src/lms/auth/repository.py:41-57 (authenticate), src/lms/auth/passwords.py:46-62 (verify_password), src/lms/auth/login.py:124-150 (login_submit + anti-enumeration comment), render.yaml:32-39 (AUTH_REQUIRED=true, ENABLE_LOCAL_IDENTITY_ROUTES=false). Measured timing delta ~30 ms (Argon2id verify) vs ~0 ms (no-user branch). No rate limiting exists in the codebase. Refinement to the suggested fix: a fully constant-time authenticate() should also equalize the two early-exit sub-paths that remain after the dummy-verify is added — (a) the password_hash is None case (local-dev user, where verify_password short-circuits to False without hashing) and (b) the needs_rehash/rehash branch on success — otherwise a smaller secondary timing signal persists. The dummy-verify fix the reporter proposes closes the dominant (exists vs not-exists) channel, which is the main finding.

confirmed.title: No rate limiting or account lockout on /login — unlimited online password guessing
